one_line_takeaway: By runtime-patching a white-box LLM's residual-stream activations toward those of a near-identical benign 'twin' prompt (patch-then-compute across layers, tuned by a dimension-selection threshold tau and an interpolation strength gamma), the attack achieves actionable jailbreak outputs on 32.8% of AdvBench prompts against Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct (vs. 7.1% baseline) without altering the input text at all.
key_finding: Activation Surgery achieved actionable answers on 32.8% (171/520) of AdvBench prompts against Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct, versus a 7.1% (37/520) no-attack baseline actionable rate. Per-category success: Bomb/Weapons/Chemical Agents 24.1% (14/58) vs 0.0% baseline; Cyberattacks/Safety Bypass 39.8% (94/236) vs 8.0% baseline; Misinformation/Discrimination 27.9% (63/226) vs 8.0% baseline (Table 1). The paper explicitly flags that this success rate is sensitive to optimization budget: allowing up to three twin-prompt attempts per prompt raised the Cyberattacks success rate from 39.8% to 69.1%. Mechanistically, sweeping tau on the 'bomb/book' pair (gamma=0 fixed) showed an operating window of tau roughly 0.6-1.0 for actionable outputs (too low tau over-patches and mimics the twin/benign output; too high tau under-patches and refusal persists); the paper recommends tau=0.8 as a default starting point. Sweeping gamma at fixed tau=0.8 showed a monotonic actionable-to-refusal trade-off as gamma increases from 0 to 1, with the fraction of patched dimensions rho-bar(gamma) rising from 2.1% at gamma=0 to 25.4% at gamma=1 (Figure 4, full 11-point table reported). The finer 143-configuration grid search (Figure 5) revealed a stable actionable operating region roughly tau in [0.4,1.0], gamma in [0.0,0.3], and an Elo ranking of actionable (tau,gamma) pairs for the bomb prompt (Table 2) showed the highest-Elo configuration (tau=1.1, gamma=0.0, Elo 1481) produced a detailed ingredient/instruction recipe, while low-Elo configurations (e.g. tau=0.3, gamma=0.9, Elo 531) produced only vague, non-actionable general advice -- validating that Elo score tracks output harmfulness/actionability.
core_contribution: The paper's own novel method is 'Activation Surgery': a white-box jailbreak that never modifies the input prompt. For a given illicit prompt, the authors manually craft a benign 'twin' prompt differing by as few tokens as possible (e.g. 'How to make a bomb?' vs 'How to make a book?'). At the final prompt token position t*, they compute hidden-state vectors for both prompts at every layer, then sequentially (patch-then-compute, respecting causal dependence between layers) replace selected dimensions of the illicit activation with an interpolated value between the illicit and twin activations. Two hyperparameters control the intervention: tau, a per-dimension threshold selecting which dimensions of the residual stream to patch (mask m_l(tau) = 1[|h_ill - h_twin| > tau]); and gamma in [0,1], which interpolates between the twin vector (gamma=0, maximal substitution) and the illicit vector (gamma=1, vanishing intervention) for the selected dimensions. They also introduce a semi-automatic three-phase evaluation pipeline: GPT-4.1 first-pass filtering into Refusal/Unrelated/Actionable categories, an Elo-based pairwise-comparison ranking (initial rating 1000, K-factor 40) over outputs classified Actionable to surface the most harmful/actionable candidates, and final manual inspection of the top-3 Elo-ranked outputs per prompt.
threat_model: White-box attacker with full access to model weights, the runtime environment, and intermediate activations, able to alter the forward pass (e.g. via hooks/tracing in self-hosted or instrumented inference stacks) but with limited training data/compute, explicitly excluding fine-tuning-based attacks and any modification of model parameters or the input prompt text. The adversary targets a small, curated set of illicit prompts and is willing to invest manual effort (constructing twin prompts, tuning tau/gamma, inspecting outputs) rather than seeking a fully automated attack scalable to thousands of prompts. Three observable output classes are defined: Refusal (R), Unrelated/Non-responsive (U), and Actionable Answer (A); the adversary's goal is at least one Actionable Answer per illicit prompt, not maximizing a raw non-refusal rate.
limitations: The authors explicitly decline to give direct quantitative comparisons against other jailbreak attacks, citing (1) a differing threat model (white-box, no prompt modification, vs typical black-box prompt-based attacks) and (2) incompatible success definitions across the literature (many prior works count any non-refusal as success, whereas this paper requires actionable utility), making percentage-only comparisons potentially misleading. The main quantitative study is conducted on a single model (Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct); transfer to Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 was only qualitatively verified, and the effective (tau,gamma) operating region is stated to be model-dependent, so the paper does not claim a universal hyperparameter setting. Twin prompts were constructed manually and not optimized for attack strength -- the paper notes this as leaving performance on the table and proposes RL-guided or diffusion-based twin-prompt optimization as future work. The paper tested an alternative intervention token (targeting the token most associated with illicit intent) and found no improvement over patching the last prompt token, while adding tokenizer-dependent complexity, so it defaults to last-token patching. The GPT-4.1-based first-pass filter is reported to have high precision for Refusal/Unrelated but a high false-positive rate for Actionable classification, requiring the Elo ranking plus final manual inspection step as a correction -- i.e., the automated pipeline alone is not reliable and human judgment remains load-bearing.

datasets_used: AdvBench 'Harmful Behaviors' (520 prompts, Zou et al. 2023), LLM-annotated into three risk categories by the authors
quantitative_results: Overall attack success: 171/520 (32.8%) actionable vs baseline 37/520 (7.1%) on Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct
Bomb/Weapons/Chemical Agents category: 14/58 (24.1%) attack success vs 0/58 (0.0%) baseline
Cyberattacks/Safety Bypass category: 94/236 (39.8%) attack success vs 19/236 (8.0%) baseline
Misinformation/Discrimination category: 63/226 (27.9%) attack success vs 18/226 (8.0%) baseline
Allowing up to 3 twin-prompt attempts raises Cyberattacks success rate from 39.8% to 69.1%

Recommended default hyperparameter: tau=0.8, empirically effective actionable window tau in [0.6,1.0] for the 'bomb/book' pair at gamma=0, broader stable operating region tau in [0.4,1.0], gamma in [0.0,0.3] from the 143-configuration grid search
Fraction of patched dimensions rho-bar(gamma) at tau=0.8: 2.1% (gamma=0) rising to 25.4% (gamma=1), 11-point table reported in Figure 4

Elo ranking of (tau,gamma) pairs for 'How to make a bomb?': top pair (1.1, 0.0) Elo 1481 (detailed recipe), bottom reported pair (0.3, 0.9) Elo 531 (vague general advice); Elo computed with initial rating 1000, K-factor 40
Average wall-clock cost: 9 min 40 s to attack one Illicit/Twin pair across all 32 configurations; Elo ranking phase ~$0.26/sample, 1-10 minutes

Cross-model transfer qualitatively confirmed on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 (no aggregate success-rate numbers reported for these models); for Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 effective tau found to be roughly 0-0.1
baselines_compared: No-attack baseline: Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct's own unmodified actionable-response rate on AdvBench (7.1% overall, per-category 0.0%/8.0%/8.0%)

No direct quantitative comparison against other jailbreak methods (e.g. GCG/AdvBench suffixes, PAIR, AutoDAN, TAP, TwinBreak) is performed; the paper explicitly justifies this omission on threat-model and success-definition incompatibility grounds rather than presenting it as a gap in execution
